Authority, nor any person executing the Water System Bonds shall be liable personally thereon by 
reason of the issuance thereof. 
 
Service Charges and Water Revenues  
 
In accordance with the provisions of the Act, the Authority is authorized to charge and collect 
rents, rates, fees and other charges, commonly referred to as “user fees”, for the use or services of 
both the Water System and Sewer System.  Such us er fees may be charged to and collected from 
any municipality, any person contracting for such use or services, or from the owner or occupant, 
or both, of any real property connected to the Water System and Sewer System. The Act requires 
that, as nearly as the Authority may deem prac ticable, such user fees be uniform throughout the 
City for the same type, class and amount of use or service pr ovided by the Water System and 
Sewer System. Each year the Authority reviews its current rate schedules for the Water and Sewer 
Systems and adopts revised schedules, if necessary. Reference should be made to “THE WATER  
24 
  
 
 
SYSTEM” and “THE SEWER SYSTEM” herein for further discussion of the Authority rates, fees 
and other charges.   
 
Sewer Bond Reserve Fund 
 
As further security for the payment of the Se wer Bonds, the Authority has established a Sewer 
Bond Reserve Fund under the Sewer Bond Resolution. The amount to be deposited into the Sewer 
Bond Reserve Fund shall be equal to the Sewer B ond Reserve Requirement, as of any particular 
date of calculation. With the issuance of the Sewer Bonds, the Authority will be required to 
increase the amounts available within the Sewer Bond Reserve Fund. The Sewer Bond Reserve 
Requirement at the time of issuance of the Sewer Bonds will be an amount equal to Maximum 
Annual Debt Service on all Outs tanding Sewer Bonds (as such te rms are defined in the Sewer 
Bond Resolution).  
 
The Authority may satisfy all or part of the Sewer Bond Reserve Requirement by providing a letter 
of credit, surety bond, loan agreement or other cr edit agreement, facility, insurance or guarantee 
arrangement (the “Bond Reserve Credit Facility”). In the event that there is a deficiency in the 
Sewer Bond Service Fund (as defined in the Sewer Bond Resolution) to provide for any 
withdrawals therefrom as require d by the terms of the Sewer B ond Resolution, the Trustee will 
transfer funds from the Sewer Bond Reserve Fund, or draw upon any such Bond Reserve Credit 
Facility and transfer such funds, into the Sewer Bond Service Fund or the Sewer Bond Sinking 
Fund (as such terms are defined in the Sewer B ond Resolution) in such amounts as are necessary 
to make up such deficiency therein.  
 
Water Bond Reserve Fund 
 
The Water Bond Resolution requires the Authority to maintain within the Water Bond Reserve 
Fund an amount equal to the Water Bond Reserve Requirement. The Water Bond Reserve 
Requirement is defined to mean, as of the date of computation, an amount of money equal to 
Maximum Annual Debt Service on Water Bonds Outstanding. With the issuance of the Water 
System Bonds, the Authority will be required to increase the amounts available within the Water 
Bond Reserve Fund. The Water Bond Reserve Require ment at the time of issuance of the Water 
Bonds will be an amount equal to Maximum Annual Debt Service on all Outstanding Water Bonds 
(as such terms are defined in the Water Bond Resolution). 
 
The Authority may satisfy all or part of the Water Bond Reserve Requirement by providing a Bond 
Reserve Credit Facility. In the event that there is  a deficiency in the Water Bond Service Fund to 
provide for any withdrawals therefrom as required by the terms of the Water Bond Resolution, the 
Trustee will transfer funds fro m the Water Bond Reserve Fund, or draw upon any such Bond 
Reserve Credit Facility and transfer such funds , into the Water Bond Service Fund or the Water 
Bond Sinking Fund (as such terms are defined in the Water Bond Resolution) in such amounts as 
are necessary to make up such deficiency therein.  
 
  
25 
  
 
 
Sewer Service Contract and Annual Charges  
 
The Authority and the City have entered into the Sewer Service Contract in order to, among other 
things, provide security to the hol ders of obligations of the Aut hority, which were issued for or 
with respect to the Sewer System. Sewer Notes and Sewer Bonds, while outstanding, are entitled 
to the benefits of the Sewer Service Contract. The Sewer Service Contract provides in relevant 
part that if the Authority shall not collect sufficient revenues to pay and provide for the estimated 
expenses of operation and maintenance of the Sewer System and the payment of principal of and 
interest on certain bonds of the Authority issued for the Sewer System (including the Sewer Notes 
and Sewer Bonds), the City is obligated to make  payment to the Author ity of the deficiency 
amount.  In the opinion of bond co unsel to the Authority, the oblig ation of the City to make 
deficiency payments to the Authority constitutes a general obligation of the City payable, if not 
otherwise available, from ad valorem taxes levied upon all the taxable property within the City, 
without limitation as to rate or amount, with respect to any portion of the deficiency payments that 
are necessary for the Authority to meet its debt service obligations on bonds (including the Sewer 
Notes and Sewer Bonds) entitled to the benefit of the Sewer Service Contract. 
 
Pursuant to its terms, the Sewer Service Contract will remain in full force and effect as long as any 
obligations of the Authority which are entitled to the benefits thereof remain outstanding. 
 
Pursuant to the terms of the Sewer Service Cont ract, the Authority shall impose and collect User 
Charges, as such term is defined in the Sewer Service Contract, from those utilizing the services 
of the Sewer System in an amount which is suffi cient to pay and provide for, inter alia, the 
estimated expenses of (i) operation and maintenance of the Sewer System, (ii) the principal of and 
interest on certain bonds of the Authority issued for the Sewer System (including the Sewer Notes 
and Sewer Bonds), (iii) maintenance of reserves or sinking funds as may be required by the terms 
of any contract of the Author ity and the Sewer Bond Resolution, and (iv) compliance with the 
terms of any bond resolution and with the Ac t.  See “APPENDIX F – COPY OF THE SEWER 
SERVICE CONTRACT”. 
 
Pursuant to the terms of the Sewer Service Contract, the City has the right, among other things, to 
approve the undertaking of any Sewer Project by resolution of the City Council and consent of the 
Municipal Representative.  See “APPEND IX F – COPY OF THE SEWER SERVICE 
CONTRACT.” The City has consented to the undertaking of the Sewer Projects to be financed by 
the Sewer Notes and Sewer Bonds by resolutions duly adopted by the City  Council on June 14, 
2017 and October 24, 2018. 
 
Water Service Contract  
 
The Authority has been operating the Water System pursuant to a franchise from the City first 
granted in April 1998.  The City and the Authority entered into the current Water Service Contract 
as of February 1, 2024. The Water Service Contract contemplates payment by the Authority to the 
City of an annual franchise fee of $23,000,000 per year for the term of the Water Service Contract, 
and in addition requires payment by the Authority to the City of three (3) yearly, up front franchise 
fee payments in the aggregate sum of $50,000,000, comprised of a $30,000,000 up front franchise  
26 
  
 
 
fee payment in 2024 which wa s financed via the Author ity’s $35,000,000 Water Franchise 
Revenue Bonds, Series 2024 (Federally Taxa ble), a $10,000,000 up front franchise fee payment 
in 2025, which is being provided fo r, in part, through the issuan ce of the Taxable Bonds, and a 
$10,000,000 up front franchise payment in 2026, as all described in greater detail in the Water 
Service Contract. The Authority may, in 2026, issu e Additional Water Bonds to finance all or a 
part of the third up front franchise fee payments. A copy of the Water Service Contract is attached 
as Appendix E hereto. See “THE WATER SYSTEM” herein.  
 
In the opinion of bond counsel to the Authority, the obligation of the City to pay Annual Charges 
to the Authority under the Water Service Contract constitutes a general obligation of the City 
payable, if not otherwise available, from ad valorem taxes levied upon all the taxable property 
within the City, without limitation as to rate or amount, with respect to any portion of the Annual 
Charges that is necessary for the Authority to meet  its debt service obliga tions on bonds entitled 
to the benefit of the Water Service Contract, including the Water Notes and Water System Bonds.  
 
Rate Covenants  
 
Under the terms of the Sewer Resolution, the Aut hority has covenanted to make, impose, charge 
and collect Sewer Service Charges in each fiscal year in accordance with the provisions of the Act 
so that Sewer Revenues for each fiscal year will be  at least sufficient to (a) pay Sewer Operating 
Expenses in such fiscal year, (b) pay all interest on and principal of all Sewer Bonds and the First 
Lien Sewer Bonds as the same shall become due and payable without recourse to or withdrawal 
from the Sewer Bond Reserve Fund, (c) pay all other amounts that are required to be paid pursuant 
to the General Bond Resolution, and (d) provide funds in concert with Water Service Charges for 
the payments required under the Water Service Contract.  
 
Under the terms of the Water Re solution, the Authority has covena nted to make, impose, charge 
and collect Water Service Charges in accordance with the Act so that the Water Revenues for each 
Fiscal Year will be at least sufficient (1) to pay Water Operating Expenses in the Fiscal Year, (2) 
to pay all interest on and principal of all Wate r Bonds, the First Lien Water Bonds and any City 
Water Bonds as the same shall be come due and payable without re course to or withdrawal from 
the Water Bond Reserve Fund, (3) to pay all other amounts due under the Water Service Contract 
and (4) to pay all other amounts that are required to be paid  pursuant to the General Bond 
Resolution.   
 
Outstanding Sewer Bonds  
 
As of December 31, 2024, the Authorit y had outstanding (i) $13,515,000 Sewer Revenue 
Refunding Bonds, Series 2020; and (ii) $80,000,000 Sewer Project Notes, Series 2024B. The 
Authority also had issued and outstanding Se wer Bonds as of December 31, 2023 in the amount 
of $194,143,227 through the NJIB program to fund the construction of various projects at locations 
throughout the City. 
  
27 
  
 
 
 
Authority Water Bonds  
 
As of December 31, 2024, the Authority had outstanding; (i) $12,970,000 Water Revenue Bonds, 
Series 2003B; (ii) $2,660,000 Water Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2020; (iii) $50,000,000 
Water Revenue Bonds, Series 2024C; (iv) $35,000,000 Wa ter Franchise Revenue Bonds, Series 
2024D (i) $25,000,000 Water Project Notes, Series 2024A; and (vi) $25,000,000 Water Project 
Notes, Series 2024E. The Authority also had issued outstanding Water Bonds as of December 31, 
2023 in the amount of $86,126,558 through the NJIB program to fund the construction of various 
projects at locations throughout the City. 
 
City Water Bonds   
 
The Authority is responsible for th e payment of the debt service on  bonds issued by the City for 
support of the water system (the “City Water Bonds”).  As of December 31, 2024, the City has 
$4,200,000 principal amount of City Water Bonds out standing.  See Appendix A attached hereto 
for a detailed accounting of all City Water Bonds.  
 
Additional Obligations 
  
Pursuant to the terms of the General Sewer Note Resolution and the General Water Note 
Resolution, the Authority may, from time to time, i ssue one or more series of Additional Sewer 
Project Notes or Additional Water Project Notes (the “Additional Note Obligations”) for the 
purposes set forth in the Genera l Sewer Note Resolution or the Water Project Note Resolution, 
including payment of additional costs of the Water and Sewer Systems and the issuance of 
refunding notes.  Any Additional Note Obligations issued by the Authority will be issued on a 
parity basis (i.e., of equal rank, w ithout preference, priority or distinction) and will be entitled to 
the same pledge of Revenues and other moneys held under the General Sewer Note Resolution or 
the Water Project Note Resolution as applicable to the Sewer Notes and Water Notes.
 
 
Prior to or concurrently with the issuance of any series of Additional Note Obligations, the 
Authority must satisfy certain c onditions precedent that are set forth in the General Sewer Note 
Resolution or the General Water Note Resolution and the Sewer Service Contract, as applicable. 
 
Pursuant to the terms of the General Bond Resolution, the Authority may, from time to time, issue 
one or more series of Additional Bonds for the purposes set forth in the General Bond Resolution, 
including payment of additional costs of the Water and Sewer Systems and the issuance of 
refunding bonds.  Any Additional Bonds issued by th e Authority will be issued on a parity basis 
(i.e., of equal rank, without preference, priority or distinction, with the Bonds) and will be entitled 
to the same pledge of Revenue s and other moneys held under the General Bond Resolution as 
applicable to the Bonds. 
 
Prior to or concurrently  with the issuance of any series of  Additional Bonds, the Authority must 
satisfy certain conditions precedent that are set forth in the General Bond Resolution and the Sewer 
Service Contract, as applicable. 
  
28 
  
 
 
THE AUTHORITY 
 
General  
 
The Authority is a public body cor porate and politic of the Stat e, which was reorganized, in 
accordance with the provisions of the Act, and by an ordinance of the City, duly adopted on 
December 10, 1997 and approved by the Mayor of  the City on December 12, 1997.  Prior to 
reorganization, the Authority was pr eviously known as the Jersey City Sewerage Authority.  As 
part of the reorganization, the Authority, as successor to the Jersey C ity Sewerage Authority, 
operates its Sewer System (as defined in the Resolution).  The Water System is operated by Veolia 
(formerly known as Suez) (“Veolia”) pursuant to a private operating contract with the Authority 
dated February 1, 2008 (the “Veolia Agreement”) and as subsequently extended by an amendment 
between Veolia and the Authority effective May 1, 2018, which amendment, inter alia, extended 
the term of the agreement through April 30, 2027.  
 
The Authority's mailing address is 13 - 15 Linden Ave East, Jersey City, New Jersey 07305.  Its 
telephone number is (201) 432-1150. 
 
Management 
 
The Authority is governed by a board consisting of five members and two alternates, each of whom 
is appointed by the Mayor, with the advice and consent of the City Council.  Upon expiration of a 
member's term, such member shal l continue to serve un til such member has been reappointed or 
until a successor has been appointed and qualified.

In 2021 the Authority entered into an assignment agreement with th e City to take over the solid 
waste collection billing functions for the City’s contract with a private hauler. In order to pay for 
these costs, the Authority established a separate  solid waste utility with rates for customers 
benefiting from the solid waste collection service.  Revenues attributed to the solid waste utility 
are programmed to pay the direct and indirect costs the Authority incurs related to the collection 
contract and not impact either water or sewer revenues or expenses. The solid waste revenues have 
been recorded on the books of the Authority within the Se wer Utility budget for ease of 
administration.   
 
THE WATER SYSTEM 
 
Background  
 
The Authority has been operating the Water System pursuant to a franchise from the City first 
granted in April 1998. On February 1, 2024, the City and Authority entered into the Water Service 
Contract extending the water franchise for a term of 40 years until December 28, 2063, and 
updating the Authority’s payment obligations to the City thereunder. A copy of the Water Service 
Contract is included as Appendix G hereto. 
 
General  
 
The Water System consists of five (5) major co mponents: reservoirs, wate r treatment facilities, 
water transmission facilities, water distribution facilities and land.  Much of the water system 
property is located outside City limits.  All of the water for the City is primarily supplied by the 
Boonton Reservoir which is located in the Town of Boonton and the Township of Parsippany-
Troy Hills, each in Morris County, New Jersey. The Boonton Reservoir is fed by the Rockaway 
River which drains a watershed of 122 square miles. The Splitrock Reservoir, located in the 
Township of Rockaway, Morris County, New Jers ey, is an emergency source of water, which 
flows via Beaver Brook to the Rockaway River above the Boonton Reservoir, and counts toward 
the Authority’s safe yield requirement.  The water treatment plant locat ed next to the Boonton 
Reservoir was completed in 1978.  The water treatment plant capacity is approximately 80 million 
gallons per day (MGD). Its current peak usage is approximately 60 MGD while its average usage 
is 52 MGD and its safe yield is 56.8 MGD.  Water is conveyed from the Boonton Reservoir by an 
extensive gravity piping and tunn el system, approximately 23 mile s in length, to the Jersey City 
distribution system and the storag e tank at the end of Troy Street in the City.  The distribution 
system consists basically of th e Troy Street Pump Station and adjacent Reservoir Tank and the 
piping network that supplies water throughout the City and the water meters to each user. Each 
residence or apartment unit and business concern in the City must be connected to the Water 
System.  The City owns approximately 2,500 acres of land related to the Water System. 
 
The Water System is currently ope rated by Veolia pursuant to the Veolia Agreement. Veolia is 
responsible for the full operation of the Water System and currently bi lls all of the users of the 
Water System and collects all the revenue on behalf of the Authority.  Under the Veolia  
30 
  
 
 
Agreement, the Authority pays Veolia an annua l service fee for the operation, maintenance and 
management of the Water System.  The annual fee is a base fee in the amount of $17,811,540 in 
the Authority’s 2025 budget (including a maintena nce allowance or “cap ”) (on an annualized 
basis), which fee is further adjustable for infla tion in accordance with a formula contained in the 
Veolia Agreement over the term of the Veolia Agreement. A component of the annual base fee is 
an amount for annual maintenance to be undertaken by Veolia. In addition, Ve olia is entitled to 
additional fees on a sliding scale if meter change outs generate increased revenues.  
 
The Authority’s Water System operating budget includes total net appropriations of $82,305,080 
in 2025. Annual payments to the City of Jersey City and Veolia account for approximately 26.7% 
and 21.6%, respectively, of the Au thority’s budget. Payments for de bt service of the Authority 
account for approximately 14.5% of the Water System budget in 2025. 
 
Operating Contract 
 
Under the Veolia Agreement, Veolia is responsible for the management, administration, 
maintenance and repair of the Water System.  The Veolia Agreement charges Veolia with a number 
of responsibilities in connection with the operation of the Water System, including providing labor 
necessary to operate, maintain, administer and manage the Water System 24 hours per day, 7 days 
per week, providing facilities management and support, co mputer-based accounting and 
management systems, and quality assurance systems, meter reading, hydrant flushing, billing and 
collection, monthly reporting and other services. 
 
Billings and Collections.  As part of the Veolia Agreement, Veolia utilizes a billing and collection 
software program.  Currently, Veolia conducts all billing and collection in-house.     
 
Unaccounted-for Water.   The Auth ority annual computes a rate fo r unaccounted for water (i.e., 
gallons of water produced at the treatment plant compared to the gallons of water metered).  The 
rate for unaccounted for water is equal to 100% minus the accounted for water rate.  Unaccounted 
for water can result from a number of factors including leaks, unauthorized use of the system and 
inaccurate or non-working meters.  Since the Water System is repr esentative of older urban type 
systems found in the Northeast, a certain amount of unaccounted for water can be expected.  As 
of December 31, 2024 the rate of unaccounted fo r water was approximately 19% (accounted for 
water rate was approximately 81%). 
 
Meter Reading and Replacement.  One method of reducing unaccounted-for water is by improving 
the timeliness and accuracy of system meter readings.  Veolia is responsible for reading all of the 
Water System's meters.  Commerc ial and industrial accounts are read monthly and retail and 
domestic accounts bi-monthly.  Veolia is charged with improving the percentage of meter readings 
that are based upon actual reads of the meters as opposed to estimates based on previous 
consumption levels. For the period January through December 31, 2024, Veolia’s read rate 
averaged approximately 91.35%.  
    
31 
  
 
 
Shut-off Policy.  The Authority has a written shut-off policy for non-payment of water charges.  
Pursuant to the Authority’s rules and regulations, delinquent accounts can be  issued a notice that 
water service will be terminated  for non-payment of water charges or other allowed reasons.  
Delinquent customers are offered the opportunity  to enter into payment plans to clear up 
outstanding arrearages.  After the requisite noticing period, the Aut hority may decide to shut off 
service.  Certain charges incurred in connection with terminating a customer's water service would 
be added to such customer's bill and be required to be paid prior to service being restored.   
 
Sale of Excess Water.  A major component of th e Authority’s water revenues are generated from 
bulk water sales to other municipalities or water purveyors, including Veolia and its subsidiaries.  
In September 2018, Veolia (as successor to Suez Water New Jersey Inc.), the City and the 
Authority entered into an agreement pursuant to wh ich Veolia agreed to purchase not less than 7 
million gallons of water per day (the “Bulk Water Contract”).  The term of the Bulk Water Contract 
ended on August 31, 2024.  Pursuant to the terms of  the Bulk Water Contract, Veolia provided 
notice to the City and Authority that Veolia wished to negotiate the renewal of the Bulk Water 
Contract.  The parties are curren tly negotiating such renewal, and anticipate entering into a new 
Bulk Water Contract during the first half of 2025.  In the meantime, the parties continue to honor 
the terms of the original Bulk Water Contract as if  the terms of such contra ct are still in effect.  
The Authority’s other existing bulk water sales contracts are set forth in Schedule B to the Water 
Service Contract, which may change from time to time.  
 
Maintenance and Replacement Objectives.  Pursuant  to the Veolia Agreement, there has been a 
computerized maintenance management program in place since 2021.  The main benefit of such 
program is the extension of the useful lives of the Water System's major components.  This in turn 
acts to reduce the overall cost of capital replacem ent projects, and thus permit the Authority to 
allocate more of its projected capital spending programs for Water System enhancements.  Veolia 
is also required to conduct periodic predictive maintenance evaluations in order to better forecast 
maintenance needs and to carry out preventive maintenance as opposed to corrective maintenance 
after the equipment or facilities failed.   
 
Water System 5-Year Capital Improvement Program  
 
Historically, the Authority’s 5- year water capital improvement program was primarily directed 
towards replacement and repair of failed equipment (corrective maintenance) due to the lack of a 
preventative and predictive maintenance program. 
Veolia’s computerized and predictive 
maintenance programs increase the Authority’s abil ity to focus the 5-year water capital plan 
towards upgrading and improving the Water System including preparing to meet more stringent 
safe drinking water standards to be imposed in the near future. 
 
The Authority’s 5-year capital improvement prog ram for the Water System included in its 2025 
budget includes approximately $613.1 million in costs, including approximately $131.4 million in 
2025 and $121.5 million in 2026. 
  
32 
  
 
 
Schedule of Service Charges 
 
The Authority periodically adopts or amends us er charges as necessary to meet its revenue 
requirements for managing, operating, and maintaining the Water System and Sewer System.  
There are a variety of charges and fees.  The charge that generates the largest revenue is the retail 
consumption rate.  As of June 1, 2024, the retail consumption water rate is $5.08 per hundred cubic 
feet of water.  The fees for City residents for water and sewer services are on two (2) separate line 
items on one bill.  In accordance with the Auth ority’s Water and Wastew ater Rate Study dated 
May 23, 2024, the Authority anticipates adopting new rate schedules on or before July 1, 2025, 
which the Authority expects will include a 5.00% increase to such exis ting retail consumption 
water rate. See “SECURITY FOR THE NOTES — Service Charges and Revenues” herein.

THE SEWER SYSTEM 
 
General 
 
The topography of the City varies from slightly below sea level near the Hudson River and along 
Newark Bay to an elevation of about 150 feet along the Palisade Ridge that traverses the City along 
Kennedy Boulevard.  The ridge divides the City into east and west drainage basins.  Sewage flows 
from the eastern drainage basin are received at the East Side Pumping Station, and flows from the 
western portion of the City are received at the West Side Pumping Station.  The west side collection 
system receives flow from a large portion of North Bergen Township and a small section of Union 
City for sanitary and storm water needs.  The Ea st Side Pumping Station flow is pumped to the 
West Side Pumping Station and is sent for seco ndary treatment to the Passaic Valley Sewerage 
Commissioner's (“PVSC”) wastewater treatment plant in Newark, New Jersey. 
 
The collection system is primarily a combined system comprised of a network of trunk sewers, 
tide gates, regulators, netting facilities interceptors and outfall sewers.  The collector sewers range 
in size from 8 to 138 inches and va ry in age to over 50 years.  Th e East Side interceptor is 48 
inches in diameter at its Nort hern end near 15th St reet and increases to 84 inches where it 
discharges to the East Side Plant.  The Southern portion of the East Side Interceptor increases from 
42 inches at Brown Place to 66 inches where it disc harges to the East Side Plant.  On the West 
Side, the interceptor is 42 inches in diameter at its Southern end near Mina Drive, 48 inches at its 
Northern terminus near Secaucus Road and enters the West Side Plant through the 84-inch influent 
pipe.  Regulator chambers installed on the trunk sewers send wastewater to the interceptor sewers, 
and during periods of heavy rain, divert excessive flows to the surrounding waters.  The regulator 
structures and interceptor sewers were placed into operation in 1957. 
 
In December 1989, the Authority completed construction of various improvements which allowed 
the Authority to convey all sewage flows emanating from within its service area to the PVSC's  
35 
  
 
 
wastewater treatment plant in Newark for treatment and discharge.  The improvement included 
reconstruction of pumping stations and grit chambers at both the East Side and West Side Plants, 
construction of a new 54-inch crosstown force main along the existing railroad right-of-way, 
construction of a new 36-inch force main from the West Side Plant to connect to the 54-inch 
crosstown line, and construction of a new 72-inch  force main across Newark Bay to the PVSC 
plant. In 2008 and 2009, the Authority expanded the Westside Pump Station grit chamber to 
receive flow from North Bergen Township and pump to the PCSV for treatment.  In addition, 
netting facilities have been constructed on the CSO outfall pipes in accordance with a New Jersey 
Department of Environmental Protection issued permit. 
 
Sewer System 5-Year Capital Improvement Plan 
 
The Authority’s 5-year capital improvement program for the Se wer System included in its 2025 
budget includes approximately $741.2 million in costs, including approximately $119.2 million in 
2025 and $156.1 million in 2026.
 
 
Operation and Maintenance Costs  
 
The Authority’s Sewer System operating budget includes total net appropriations of $95,434,362 
in 2025. Annual payments to PVSC account for a pproximately 25% of the Sewer System budget 
in 2025.  Payment for debt service of the Authority account for approximately 8.7% of the Sewer 
System budget in 2025.  
 
User Charges  
 
The Authority's sewer revenue is derived from assessment of charges to City users, service contract 
payments from the North Hudson Sewerage Aut hority and North Bergen Municipal Utilities 
Authorities, interest on delinquent payments, investment and other miscellaneous minor sources.  
The Authority is authorized pursuant to the Act to charge and collect service charges and to 
prescribe and revise from time to time a schedule of all its service charges.  The Act provides that 
the schedule of service charges must comply with the terms of any contract of the Authority and 
must be such that the revenues of the Authority will at all times be adequate to pay the expenses 
of operation and maintenance of the sewer system, including reserves, insurance, extensions, and 
replacements, and to pay the principal of and th e interest on any bonds, and to maintain such 
reserves or sinking funds thereof as may be required by the terms of any contract of the Authority 
or as may be deemed necessary or desirable by the Authority.  The propos ed rate schedule must 
be prescribed and from time to time may be re vised by the Authority afte r a public hearing held 
by the Authority at least 20 days after a notice of the proposed adju stment is mailed to the City 
and publication of notice of the proposed adjustment of the service charges and the time and place 
of the public hearing.  The Act also provides that the Authority must present evidence at the hearing 
that the proposed adjustment is reasonable and necessary and must provide an opportunity for 
cross-examination of persons offering such evidence.  As of June 1, 2024, the retail consumption 
sewer rate was $7.01 per hundred cubic feet of wate r.  In accordance with the Authority’s Water 
and Wastewater Rate Study dated May 23, 2024, th e Authority anticipates adopting new rate  
36 
  
 
 
schedules on or before July 1, 2025, which the Authority expects will include a 5.00% increase to 
such existing retail consumption sewer rate.

United States Environmental Protection Agency Consent Decree and Modification  
 
In 2011, the Authority entered into the Consent Decree with the USEP A.  Although under the 
terms of the Consent Decree the Authority has ad mitted no liability, the USEPA has alleged that 
the Authority violated the CWA and the CSS General Permit in a number of ways, including but 
not limited to, causing dry weather overflows, failing to prope rly operate and maintain its 
Combined Sewer System (“CSS”), failing to prevent tidal intrusion into the collection system, and 
allowing the discharge of untreated sewage fro m the collection system onto public and private 
property in Jersey City. 
 
The Consent Decree mandated that the Authorit y undertake a Combined Sewer System Capacity 
and Condition Assessment Study (the “Study”) to better understand its CSS and to identify actions 
that need to be undertaken to address flooding and other proble ms in the CSS.  The Study was 
intended to enable the Authority to prioritize improvements needed and begin addressing those 
improvements in an orderly fashion as capital improvement funding became available.      
 
The Consent Decree authorized the Authority to undertake a phase d approach to the Study, with 
all phases as of the Study being completed by no  later than January 15, 2015.  In total the Study 
contains eight (8) distinct phases.  Within th ree (3) months after completing each phase of the 
Study, the Consent Decree also required the Authority to prepare, for USEPA approval, a report 
containing recommended actions to be implemented by the Authority as a result of the Study.  Each 
report was to include a timeframe for completing each recommended action.  The USEPA and the 
Authority agreed to confer on an as needed basis to discuss relative priority to be given to the 
action items resulting from the Study, with the Authority being responsi ble for completing all 
recommended actions as soon as practicable, but no later than December 31, 2021. 
 
Although the Authority has met its obligations to complete the phased Study, to submit subsequent 
reports to USEPA, and to complete implementati on of certain recommended actions in a timely 
manner, a number of significant and costly sewe r improvements that were  not anticipated when 
the Study was developed have made implementation of all recommended actions by the December 
31, 2021 deadline impracticable.  Accordingly, the Authority pr epared and submitted the 
Modification to the USEPA, to extend the d eadlines for completion of the pending phases 
recommended by the Study, which was subsequently approved by the United States District Court 
for the District of New Jersey.  To date, the Authority has completed phases 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 of the 
study, with sub-phases 2A, 5A and 5B and Phases 6, 7 and 8 (including all applicable subphases) 
remaining outstanding.
i  Pursuant to the terms of the M odification, the Aut hority has various 
deadlines to complete the work outlined therein, with the last deadline being December 31, 2031.  
38 
  
 
 
 
THE CITY OF JERSEY CITY 
 
The City is located in Hudson County, New Jersey. The City has never defaulted in the payment 
of its general obligation bonds.  See Appendices B and C attached  hereto for certain general 
information and selected financial information provided by the City concerning the City. 
 
 
SUMMARY OF LOCAL AUTHORITIES FISCAL CONTROL LAW 
 
The Local Authorities Fiscal Control Law, Chapter 313 of the Pamphlet Laws of 1983 of the State, 
became effective on November 24, 1983.  This law provides for “State review of project financing 
of local authorities and for State supervision over the financial operations of local authorities”. 
 
The Local Finance Board, in the Division of Local Government Services, Department of 
Community Affairs, New Jersey (the “Local Fina nce Board”), prescribes the procedures for the 
adoption and execution of annua l budgets, and approval must be obtained prior to a budget's 
adoption.  Such budget must also comply with the terms and provisions of any bond resolutions.  
On granting approval of a budget, the reasonableness and accuracy of revenue estimates are 
considered.  Such revenue must be sufficient to meet all expenses, including debt service.  An 
annual audit must be made and completed within four (4) months of the close of a fiscal year by a 
registered municipal accountant or certified public accountant licensed in the State. 
 
A financing program must be submitted to the Local Finance Board for a hearing and review prior 
to implementation.  Such revi ew generally focuses on the na ture, purpose and scope of the 
financing, engineering or feasib ility studies, terms and provision s of service contracts, bond 
resolutions and proposed terms and conditions of negotiated sales, and proposed or maximum debt 
service, and operational funding requirements. 
 
A local authority may not be created unless the Local Finance Board so approves and a local 
authority may not be dissolved without providing  for payment of all outstanding obligations and 
without approval by the Local Finance Board. 
 
Local Finance Board Findings  
 
The Local Finance Board issued positive findings regarding the issuance of the Water Notes on 
October 25, 2023 and December 11, 2024, the Sewer Notes and Sewer Bonds on February 9, 2022, 
and the Water Bonds on October 26, 2023.  
 
On October 25, 2023, the Local Finance Board held a hearing to consid er the Authority's 
application in connection with the issuance of the Taxable Bonds to  fund the initial franchise fee 
payable to the City in connection with a proposed  40-year extension of the Authority's right to 
operate and maintain the Water System. Afte r testimony from the Au thority's staff and 
professionals, the Local Finance Board expressed concerns that the upfront fee was excessive and 
would have a negative impact on ratepayers.  As a result, the Local Finance Board issued negative  
39 
  
 
 
findings with regard to this issue. Pursuant to N.J.S.A. 40A:5-7, upon receipt of the Local Finance 
Board’s resolution memorializing such findings, the Authority shall, within 45 days of receipt of 
the Local Finance Board’s findings resolution, ce rtify, by resolution to the Local Finance Board, 
that each member of the Authority has personally reviewed such findings and will evidence same 
by group affidavit signed by a majority of the full membership of the Authority.  To date, the Local 
Finance Board has not yet delivered such resolution to the Authority. 
 
PLEDGE OF STATE NOT TO LIMIT POWERS OF 
AUTHORITY OR RIGHTS OF HOLDERS OF OBLIGATIONS 
 
The Act sets forth the pledge and agreement of the State that it will not limit or alter the rights 
vested by the Act in the Authority  to fulfill the terms of any agreements which have been made 
with the holders of the Authority's obligations or in any way impair the rights and remedies of such 
holders, until such obligations, together with the interest thereon, interest on any unpaid 
installments of interest, and a ll costs and expenses in connection with any action or proceedings 
by or on behalf of such holders, are fully met and discharged. 
 
ABSENCE OF MATERIAL LITIGATION 
 
The Authority  
 
There is no controversy or litigation of any na ture now pending or threatened, restraining or 
enjoining the issuance, sale, execution of delivery of the Obligations or in any way questioning or 
affecting the validity of the Obligations or any proceedings of the Authority taken with respect to 
the issuance or sale thereof. There is no contro versy or litigation of an y nature now pending or 
threatened, restraining or enjoining the security  pledged for the Obligations, or the pledge or 
application of any moneys or security provid ed for the payment of the Obligations, or the 
authorization, execution or enforcement of the Water Service Contract or the Sewer Service 
Contract. 
 
Rockaway Valley Regional Sewerage Authority Litigation 
 
The Authority and the City are in litigation file d in N.J. Superior Court in January 2010 (the 
“Litigation”) adverse to Rockaway Valley Regi onal Sewerage Authority (“RVRSA”) and the 
Town of Dover, Town of Boonton, Borough of Rockaway, Township of Rockaway, Township of 
Denville, Township of Randolph, Randolph Township Municipal Utilities Authority, Borough of 
Victory Gardens, Township of Boonton and Bo rough of Wharton (coll ectively, the “Municipal 
Defendants”). 
 
This lawsuit challenges the continued validity of certain 1971 and 1984 settlement agreements, 
which agreements require the Authority to contribute millions of dollars per year to the capital and 
operating expenses of the RVRSA, which treats the Municipal Defendants’ sewage.  The specific 
claims asserted in the case are: (i) a declaratory judgment that the settlement agreements are void; 
(ii) a declaratory judgment that the Authority’s obligation to contribute towards RVRSA’s capital 
costs has terminated; and (iii) damages as a re sult of RVRSA’s breach of the 1984 settlement  
40 
  
 
 
agreement.  The primary relief sought by the Author ity in the Litigation is to void the settlement 
agreements. Aside from the equitable relief sought, the Authority is seeking monetary damages in 
the approximate amount of $452,000, plus interest. 
 
The case has been extensively litigated and mediated.    
 
Settlement negotiations have failed, and the case went to trial over 13 days from October 2017 to 
June 2018.  The Court entered a partial, non-fina l judgment on liability in October 2018, which 
ruled that the Authority is responsible to contribute to RVRSA’s operating and maintenance costs 
but not RVRSA’s enlargement, ex pansion, improvement or upgrade costs.  This ruling required 
another round of extensive discovery and a second trial over six (6) days in 2021 to determine if 
any damages are owed to RVRSA.  After the sec ond trial, the court entered a judgment in the 
Authority’s favor, dismissing RVRSA’s Counterclaim.  The Authority, RVRSA and the Municipal 
Defendants have each filed appeal s from the trial court’s decisions on liability and damages.  
Pending the outcome of the appeals, the Author ity is not liable to RVRSA for the amounts 
allegedly owed for unpaid capita l assessments from 2010-2018.  Th e validity of th e settlement 
agreements will also be reviewed by the appellate court.   
     
During the pendency of the Litigation, the Author ity has been paying RVRSA’s assessments for 
operating and maintenance expenses  but is withholding payment of RVRSA’s assessed amounts 
for capital costs.  According to RVRSA, the amount of its assessed capital costs for the years 2010-
2018 that JCMUA will owe RVRSA if the Authority  does not prevail in the Litigation is 
approximately $5.7 million.  Also, in earl y 2022, RVRSA demanded payment for amounts 
RVRSA alleges the Authority owes for operating  expenses during the period of 2018 to 2021, in 
the total amount of $2,000,161.90.  The Authority re sponded to that demand and advanced the 
position that RVRSA has mischaracterized the cost s for which it is demanding payment and that 
the Authority will accordingly not make payment for those costs unless and until RVRSA provides 
the Authority with an accurate calculation of the operating costs that do not include enlargement, 
expansion, improvement or upgr ade costs.  On October 25,  2022, RVRSA filed a Complaint 
against the Authority and the City seeking approximately $2,500,000 in what RVRSA alleges are 
operating expenses owed for the period from 2018 through the filing of the 2022 Complaint.  the 
Authority moved to dismiss or st ay the 2022 Complaint on the basi s that it is dup licative of the 
prior action between the parties.  The Court denied the Authority’s motion to dismiss or stay, but 
the motion resulted in RVRSA amending its Complaint to be more specific as to its claims and to 
eliminate the duplicative allegations.  The parties have mainly completed fact discovery and are 
currently in the expert discove ry phase.  The court has schedul ed a trial date of July 28, 
2025.  RVRSA has made a proposal to have an independent engineer de termine if the RVRSA 
project costs are chargeable to the Authority, without prejudice to the Appellate Divisions pending 
decision.  The Authority and the City are considering that proposal, while awaiting the Appellate 
Division’s decision on the appeals and completing expert discovery, in the event that the trial must 
proceed.  Depending on the outcome of the pending appeals, specifically if the Appellate Division 
adopts the Authority arguments, then RVRSA’s new action is likely to be moot.  In the meantime, 
the parties will continue with the new litig
 ation relating to RVRSA’s claims.   
  
41 
  
 
 
In the opinion of the Authority’s counsel, if the Authority does not  prevail in the Litigation, it 
would not have a material impact on the financial stability or results of operations of the Authority. 
 
The City  
 
In the opinion of Corporation Counsel to the Cit y, there is no litigation pending or, to the best 
knowledge of the Corporation Counsel to the City, after due inquir y, threatened against the City 
in any court questioning the exis tence of, or which would materially adversely affect the future 
financial or operational viability of the City or the Authority, or would restrain or enjoin the 
authorization, issuance, sale or de livery of the Obligations or any proceedings of the City or the 
Authority taken with respect thereto, or the pled ge or application of any moneys, securities or 
revenues for the repayment of the Obligations, or  the authorization, execution or enforcement of 
the Sewer Service Contract or the Water Service Contract or the title of any City Council members 
or any members of the Authority to their respective offices or the outcome of which would 
materially adversely affect the financial position of the City.  See, however, “APPENDIX B – 
CERTAIN INFORMATION REGARDING THE CITY OF JERSEY CITY - LITIGATION” for 
certain information regarding litigation concerning the City.  
 
TAX MATTERS 
 
Exclusion of Interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations from Gross Income for Federal 
Tax Purposes 
 
The Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended (the "Code"), imposes certain requirements that 
must be met on a continuing basis subsequent to the issuance of the Tax-Exempt Obligations in 
order to assure that interest on the Tax-Exempt Oblig ations will be excl uded from gross income 
for federal income tax purposes under Section 103 of the Code.  Failure of the Authority to comply 
with such requirements may cause interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations to lose the exclusion 
from gross income for federal income tax purposes, retroactive to the date of issuance of the Tax-
Exempt Obligations.  The Authority will make cer tain representations in its Arbitrage and Tax 
Certificates, which will be executed on the date of issuance of the Tax-Exempt Obligations, as to 
various tax requirements.  The Authority has covenanted to comply  with the provisions of the 
Code applicable to the Tax-Exempt Obligations and has covenanted not to take any action or fail 
to take any action that would cause interest on th e Tax-Exempt Obligations to lose the exclusion 
from gross income under Section 10 3 of the Code.  Bond Counsel (a s defined herein) will rely 
upon the representations made in the Arbitrage an d Tax Certificates and will assume continuing 
compliance by the Authority with the above covenants in rendering its federal income tax opinions 
with respect to the exclusion of  interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations from gross income for 
federal income tax purposes and with respect to the treatment of interest on the Tax-Exempt 
Obligations for the purposes of alternative minimum tax. 
 
Assuming the Authority observes its covenants with respect to compliance with the Code, 
McManimon, Scotland & Baumann, LLC, Bond Counsel to the Authority ("Bond Counsel"), is of 
the opinion that, under existing law, interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations is excluded from gross 
income of the owners thereof for federal income tax purposes pursuant to Section 103 of the Code,  
42 
  
 
 
and interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations is not an item of tax preference under Section 57 of 
the Code for purposes of com puting alternative minimum tax;  however, interest on the Tax-
Exempt Obligations is included in the "adj usted financial statement income" of certain 
corporations that are subject to alternative minimum tax under Section 55 of the Code. 
 
The opinion of Bond Counsel is based on current legal authority and cove rs certain matters not 
directly addressed by such authority.  It represents Bond Counsel's legal judgment as to exclusion 
of interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations from gross income for federal income tax purposes but 
is not a guaranty of that conc lusion.  The opinion is not bindi ng on the Internal Revenue Service 
("IRS") or any court.  Bond Counsel expresses no opinion about (i) the effect of future changes in 
the Code and the applicable regulations under the Code or (ii) the interpretation and enforcement 
of the Code or those regulations by the IRS. 
 
Bond Counsel's engagement with respect to the Ta x-Exempt Obligations ends with the issuance 
of the Tax-Exempt Obligations, and, unless separately engaged, Bond Counsel is not obligated to 
defend the Authority or the owners of the Tax-Ex empt Obligations regard ing the tax status of 
interest thereon in the event of an audit examination by the IRS.  The IRS has a program to audit 
tax-exempt obligations to determine whether the interest thereon is includible in gross income for 
federal income tax purposes.  If the IRS does audit the Tax-Exempt Obligations, under current IRS 
procedures, the IRS will treat the Authority as the taxpayer and the beneficial owners of the Tax-
Exempt Obligations will have only limited rights, if any, to obtain and participate in judicial review 
of such audit.  Any action of th e IRS, including, but not  limited to, selecti on of the Tax-Exempt 
Obligations for audit, or the course or result of  such audit, or an audit of other obligations 
presenting similar tax issues, may affect the market value of the Tax-Exempt Obligations. 
 
Payments of interest on tax-exempt obligatio ns, including the Tax-Exempt Obligations, are 
generally subject to IRS Form 1099-INT informa tion reporting requirements.  If a Tax-Exempt 
Obligation owner is subject to backup withholdi ng under those requirements, then payments of 
interest will also be subject to backup withholding.  Those requirements do not affect the exclusion 
of such interest from gross income for federal income tax purposes. 
 
Original Issue Discount 
 
Certain maturities of the Tax-Exempt Obligations may be sold at an initial offering price less than 
the principal amount payable on such Tax-Exempt Obligations at maturity (the "Discount 
Obligations").  The difference between the initial public offering price of the Discount Obligations 
at which a substantial amount of  each of the Discount Obligati ons was sold and the principal 
amount payable at maturity of each of the Discount Obligations constitutes the original issue 
discount.  Bond Counsel is of the opinion that the appropriate portion of the original issue discount 
allocable to the original and each subsequent owner of the Discount Obligations will be treated for 
federal income tax purpos es as interest not incl udable in gross income under Section 103 of the 
Code to the same extent as stated interest on the Discount Obligations.  Under Section 1288 of the 
Code, the original issue discount  on the Discount Obligations accr ues on the basis of economic 
accrual.  The basis of an initia l pu
rchaser of a Discount Obligati on acquired at th e initial public  
43 
  
 
 
offering price of the Discount Obligations wi ll be increased by the amount of such accrued 
discount.  Owners of the Discount Obligations should consult their own tax advisors with respect 
to the determination for federal income tax pur poses of the original issue discount properly 
accruable with respect to the Discount Obligati ons and the tax accounting treatment of accrued 
interest. 
 
Original Issue Premium 
 
Certain maturities of the Tax-Exempt Obligations may be sold at an initial offering price in excess 
of the amount payable at the maturity date (the "Premium Obligations").  The excess, if any, of the 
tax basis of the Premium Obligations to a purch aser (other than a pu rchaser who holds such 
Premium Obligations as inventory, as stock-in-trade or for sale to customers in the ordinary course 
of business) over the amount paya ble at maturity is amortizable  bond premium, which is not 
deductible from gross income for federal income tax purposes.  Amortizable bond premium, as it 
amortizes, will reduce the owner's tax cost of th e Premium Obligations used to determine, for 
federal income tax purposes, the amount of gain or loss upon th e sale, redemption at maturity or 
other disposition of the Premium Obligations.  A ccordingly, an owner of a Premium Obligation 
may have taxable gain from the disposition of the Premium Obligation, even though the Premium 
Obligation is sold, or disposed of, for a price e qual to the owner's origin al cost of acquiring the 
Premium Obligation.  Bond premium amortizes over the term of the Premium Obligations under 
the "constant yield method" desc ribed in regulations interpre ting Section 1272 of the Code.  
Owners of the Premium Obligations should consult their own tax advisors with respect to the 
calculation of the amount of bond premium that will be treated for federal income tax purposes as 
having amortized for any taxable year (or portion thereof) of the owner and with respect to other 
federal, state and local tax consequences of owning and disposing of the Premium Obligations. 
 
Bank-Qualification 
 
The Tax-Exempt Obligations will not be designated as qualified under Section 265 of the Code by 
the Authority for an exemption from the denial of deduction for interest paid by financial 
institutions to purchase or to carry tax-exempt obligations. 
 
The Code denies the interest deduction for certain indebte dness incurred by banks, thrift 
institutions and other financial in stitutions to purchase or to ca rry tax-exempt obligations.  The 
denial to such institutions of 100% of the deduction for interest paid on funds allocable to tax-
exempt obligations applies to t hose tax-exempt obligations acquire d by such institutions after 
August 7, 1986.  For certain issues that are eligible to be designa ted, and that are designated, by 
the issuer as qualified under Section 265 of the Code, 80% of su ch interest may be deducted as a 
business expense by such institutions. 
 
Additional Federal Income Tax Consequences of Holding the Tax-Exempt Obligations 
 
Prospective purchasers of the Tax-Exempt Obligations should be aware that ownership of, accrual 
or receipt of interest on or disposition of tax-exempt obligations, such as the Tax-Exempt  
44 
  
 
 
Obligations, may have additional federal inco me tax consequences for certain taxpayers, 
including, without limitation, taxpayers eligible for the earned income credit, recipients of certain 
Social Security and certain Railroad Retirement be nefits, taxpayers that may be deemed to have 
incurred or continued indebtedness to purchas e or carry tax-exempt obligations, financial 
institutions, property and casualty companies, foreign corporations and certain S corporations. 
 
Bond Counsel expresses no opinion regarding any federal tax consequences other than its opinion 
with regard to the exclusion of interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations from gross income pursuant 
to Section 103 of the Code and interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations not constituting an item of 
tax preference under Section 57 of the Code.  Prospective purchasers of the Tax-Exempt 
Obligations should consult their tax advisors with respect to all other tax consequences (including, 
but not limited to, those listed above) of holding the Tax-Exempt Obligations. 
 
Changes in Federal Tax Law Regarding the Tax-Exempt Obligations 
 
Legislation affecting tax-exempt obligations is regularly considered by the United States Congress 
and may also be considered by the State of New Jersey.  Court proceedings may also be filed, the 
outcome of which could modify the tax treatm ent of obligations such  as the Tax-Exempt 
Obligations.  There can be no assurance that legislation enacted or proposed, or actions by a court, 
after the date of issuance of the Tax-Exempt Obligations will not have an adverse effect on the tax 
status of interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations or the market value or marketability of the Tax-
Exempt Obligations.  These adverse effects could result, for example, from changes to federal or 
state income tax rates, changes in the structur e of federal or state in come taxes (including 
replacement with another type of tax) or repeal (or reduction in the benefit) of the exclusion of 
interest on the Tax-Exempt Obligations from gross income for federal or state income tax purposes 
for all or certain taxpayers. 
 
Taxable Obligations 
 
General 
 
Interest on the Taxable Water Notes and the Taxable Bonds (together, the “Taxable Obligations”) 
is includable in gross income for federal income tax purposes. 
 
The following is a summary of certain United St ates federal income tax consequences of the 
ownership of the Taxable Obligations as of the da te hereof.  Each prospe ctive investor should 
consult with its own tax advisor regarding the application of United States federal income tax laws, 
as well as any state, local, foreign or other tax laws, to its particular situation. 
 
This summary is based on the Code, as well as Treasury Regulations and administrative and 
judicial rulings and practice.  Legislative, judicial and administrative changes may occur, possibly 
with retroactive effect, that could alter or modi fy the continued validity of the statements and 
conclusions set forth herein.  This summary is intended as a general explanatory discussion of the 
consequences of holding the Taxable Obligati ons generally and does not purport to furnish  
45 
  
 
 
information in the level of detail or with the investor's specific tax circumstances that would be 
provided by an investor's own tax advisor.  For example, this summary is addressed only to original 
purchasers of the Taxable Obligations that are "U.S. holders" (as defined below), deals only with 
the Taxable Obligations held as a capital asset within  the meaning of Section 1221 of the Code 
and does not address tax consequen ces to holders that may be rele vant to investors subject to 
special rules.  In addition, this summary does no t address alternative minimum tax issues or the 
indirect consequences to a holder of an equity interest in the Taxable Obligations. 
 
As used herein, a "U.S. holder" is a "U.S. person" th at is a beneficial owner of the Taxable 
Obligations.  A "non-U.S. investor " is a holder (or bene ficial owner) of the Taxable Obligations 
that is not a U.S. person.  For these purposes, a "U.S. person" is a citizen or resident of the United 
States, a corporation or partnership created or organized in or under the laws of the United States 
or any political subdivision thereof (except, in th e case of a partnership, to  the extent otherwise 
provided in Treasury Regulations), an estate the income of which is subject to United States federal 
income taxation regardless of its source or a trust if (i) a United States court is able to exercise 
primary supervision over the trust' s administration, and (ii) one or  more U.S. persons have the 
authority to control all of the trust's substantial decisions. 
 
Sale or Redemption of the Taxable Obligations  
 
An owner's tax basis for the Taxable Obligations is the price such owner pays for the Taxable 
Obligations plus amounts of any original issue discount included in income, reduced on account 
of any payments received (other than "qualified periodic intere st" payments) and any amortized 
premium.  Gain or loss recognized on a sale, exchange or redemption of the Taxable Obligations, 
measured by the difference between the amount realized and the Taxable Obligations' basis as so 
adjusted, will generally give  rise to capital gain or loss if th e Taxable Obligations are held as a 
capital asset. 
 
Possible Recognition of Taxable Gain or Loss Upon Defeasance of Taxable 
Obligations 
 
Defeasance of any Taxable Obligations may re sult in a deemed exchange under Section 1001 of 
the Code, in which event the holder of such Ta xable Obligations will recognize taxable gain or 
loss in an amount equal to the difference between the amount realized from the deemed exchange 
(less any accrued qualified stated interest that will be taxable as  such) and the holder's adjusted 
basis in such Taxable Obligations. 
 
Backup Withholding 
 
An owner of the Taxable Obligations may, under cer tain circumstances, be subject to "backup 
withholding" (currently the rate of this withholding tax is 24%, but may change in the future) with 
respect to interest or original  issue discount on the Taxable Obligations.  This withholding 
generally applies if the owner of the Taxable Oblig ations (a) fails to furnish the Authority or its 
paying agent with its taxpayer identification number; (b) furnishes the Authority or its paying agent  
46 
  
 
 
an incorrect taxpayer identification number; (c) fails to report properly interest, dividends or other 
"reportable payments" as defined in the Code; or (d) under certain circumstances, fails to provide 
the Authority or its paying agent with a certified  statement, signed under pe nalty of perjury, that 
the taxpayer identification number provided is its correct number and that the holder is not subject 
to backup withholding.  Backup withholding will not apply, however, with respect to certain 
payments made to Taxable Obligation owners, in cluding payments to certain exempt recipients 
(such as certain exempt organizations) and to certain Nonresidents (as defined below).  Owners of 
the Taxable Obligations should consult their tax a dvisors as to their qua lification for exemption 
from backup withholding and the procedure for obtaining the exemption. 
 
The amount of "reportable paymen ts" for each calendar year and th e amount of tax withheld, if 
any, with respect to payments on the Taxabl e Obligations will be reported to the Taxable 
Obligation owners and to the IRS. 
 
Foreign Taxable Obligation Owners 
 
Under the Code, interest and original issue discount income with respect to the Taxable 
Obligations held by nonresident alien individuals, foreign corporations or other non-United States 
persons ("Nonresidents") generally will not be subject to the United States withholding tax (or 
backup withholding) if the Authority or its paying agent (or other person who would otherwise be 
required to withhold tax from such payments) is provided with an appropriate statement that the 
beneficial owner of the Taxable Obligations is a Nonresident.  The withholding tax may be reduced 
or eliminated by an applicable tax treaty, if any.  Notwithstanding the foregoing, if any such 
payments are effectively connected with a Un ited States trade or business conducted by a 
Nonresident owner of the Taxable Obligations, they will be subject to regular United States income 
tax, but will ordinarily be exempt from United States withholding tax. 
 
ERISA 
 
The Employees Retirement Income Security Act of 1974, as amended ("ERISA"), and the Code 
generally prohibit certain trans actions between a qualified em ployee benefit plan under ERISA 
(an "ERISA Plan") and persons who, with respect to  that plan, are fiduciaries or other "parties in 
interest" within the meaning of ERISA or "disqualified persons" within the meaning of the Code.  
All fiduciaries of ERISA Plans, in  consultation with their advisors , should carefully consider the 
impact of ERISA and the Code on an investment in the Taxable Obligations. 
 
In all events, all investors should consult their own tax advisors in determining the federal, state, 
local and other tax consequences to them of the purchase, ownership and disposition of the Taxable 
Obligations. 
 
 
 
 
  
47 
  
 
 
State Taxation 
 
Bond Counsel is of the opinion that, based upon existing law, interest on the Obligations and any 
gain on the sale thereof are not included in gross income under the New Jersey Gross Income Tax 
Act.

SECONDARY MARKET DISCLOSURE 
 
In accordance with the provisions of Rule 15c2-12, as amended (the “Rule”), promulgated by the 
Securities and Exchange Commission under the Secu rities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, 
concurrently with the issuance of the Obligations, the Authority and the City will enter into 
Continuing Disclosure Agreements with Digital Assurance Certification LLC, Orlando Florida, as 
dissemination agent, in substantially the forms annexed hereto as Appendix H (the “Continuing 
Disclosure Agreements”). The Authority and the City have covenanted for the benefit of the 
Obligation holders, in accordance with the provis ions of the Continuing Disclosure Agreements, 
to provide or cause to be provided, in accord ance with the requirements of the Rule, certain 
financial information and notice of the occurrence of certain enumerated events, to the Municipal 
Securities Rulemaking Board (t he “MSRB”) through its Electr onic Municipal Market Access 
(“EMMA”) system as provided for in the Continuing Disclosure Agreements.  
 
A failure by the Authority and/or the City to comply with the provisions of the Continuing 
Disclosure Agreements will not constitute a de fault under the Obligations, and holders and 
beneficial owners are limited to the remedies se t forth in the Continuing Disclosure Agreements. 
However, failure by the Authority or the City to comply with the Continuing Disclosure 
Agreements must be reported in accordance with  the Rule and must be considered by any broker 
or dealer before recommending the purchase or sale of the Obligations in the secondary market. 
Consequently, such failure may adversely affect the transferability and liquidity of the Obligations 
and their market price. 
 
There can be no assurance that th ere will be a secondary market for the sale or purchase of the 
Obligations.  Such factors as prevailing market conditions, financial condition or market position of 
firms who may make the secondary market and the financial condition of the Authority may affect 
the future liquidity of the Obligations.

The Authority 
 
The Authority failed to comply with prior undertakings to timely provide its audited financial 
statements for the fiscal years ended Decem ber 31, 2019, and 2020. A notice of late filing has 
since been posted to EMMA on April 4, 2022. The Authority also failed to comply with prior 
undertakings to timely provide its financial operating data for the fiscal years ended December 31,  
2019 and 2020. A notice of late filing has since been posted to EMMA on April 4, 2022. The 
Authority has implemented certain procedures to file its Annual Reports on a more consistent and 
timely basis in future years.
 
 
The City  
 
The City has entered into prio r undertakings to provide contin uing disclosure for certain 
outstanding issuances. In connection with such unde rtakings, the City has filed audited financial 
statements and other financial documents with the MSRB’s Electronic Municipal Market Access 
(“EMMA”) website as required in accordance w ith the Rule. Certain ope rating data items were 
either filed in a different format than in previ ous filings, or not filed (for only one bond issue in 
2003) because said information is no longer available or no longer produced. The City has failed 
to timely file, on its own behalf and on behalf of the Jersey City Redevelopment Agency, its 2021, 
2022, and 2023 Operating Data. The 2021, 2022, and 2023 Operating Data has since been filed 
with MSRB. The City has impleme nted certain procedures to fi le its Annual Reports on a more 
consistent and timely basis in future years. The City’s implementation of procedures to file its 
Annual Reports also includes the continued utilization of Digital Assurance Certification, LLC to 
assist with the City’s disclosure requirements. The City failed to time ly file notices of the 
incurrence of financial obligations relating to (i) two lease-purchase transactions that closed in 
2019 and 2020; and (ii) a Jersey City Municipal Utilities Authority financing with the New Jersey 
Infrastructure Bank that closed in June 2021. Notices have since been filed with the MSRB.  
 
 
MUNICIPAL BANKRUPTCY 
 
The undertaking of the Authority and the City should be considered with reference to Chapter 9 
of the United States Bankruptcy Code (the “B ankruptcy Code”), 11 U.S.C. Section 901 to 946.  
Under Chapter 9 of the Bankruptcy Code, a municipality, a political subdivision or a public agency 
or instrumentality of the State that is insolvent or  unable to meet its debts may file a petition in a 
United States Bankruptcy Court (the “Bankruptcy C ourt”) to adjust its debts.  Chapter 9 of the 
Bankruptcy Code does not permit such entity to liquida te its assets and distribute the proceeds of 
its assets to its creditors.  Chapter 9 of the Bankruptcy Code permits a financially distressed public 
entity to seek protection from its creditors by staying the commencement or continuation of certain 
actions against such public entity while it formul ates and negotiates a plan of adjustment of its 
debts which can be binding on a dissenting minority of creditors if it is acceptable to the majority 
of creditors.   
 
Reference should also be made to N.J.S.A. 52:27-40 et seq., which provides that “any political 
subdivision” of the State as defined therein has the power to file a peti tion with the Bankruptcy 
Court under Chapter 9 of the Bankruptcy Code pr ovided the “political subdivision” has obtained  
49 
  
 
 
approval of the Local Finance Board, successor to the Municipal Finance Commission.  Section 
903 of the Bankruptcy Code, 11 U.S.C. Section 903, sp ecifically provides that Chapter 9 of the 
Bankruptcy Code does not limit or  impair the power of a state to  control, by legislation or 
otherwise, a municipality of or in such state in the exercise of the political or governmental powers 
of such municipality; provided, however, that a state law prescribing a method of composition of 
indebtedness of the municipali ty may not bind any creditor that  does not consent to such 
composition and that a judgment entered under such state law may not bind a creditor that does 
not consent to such composition. 
 
THE ABOVE REFERENCES TO THE BANKRUPTCY CODE ARE NOT TO BE 
CONSTRUED AS AN INDICATION THAT THE AUTHORITY OR THE CITY EXPECTS TO 
RESORT TO THE PROVISIONS OF SUCH BANKRUPTCY CODE OR THAT, IF EACH DID, 
SUCH ACTION WOULD BE APPROVED BY TH E LOCAL FINANCE BOARD, OR THAT 
ANY PROPOSED PLAN WOULD INCLUDE A DILUTION OF THE SOURCES OF 
PAYMENT OF AND SECURITY FOR THE OBLIGATIONS.

will be issued by BAM.  
 
Such ratings are not a recommend ation to buy, sell or hold any of the Obligations.  There is no 
assurance that any such ratings will be continued for any given period of time or will not be revised 
downward or withdrawn entirely by the ratings ag encies if, in their respective judgment, 
circumstances so warrant.  Any such downward revision or withdrawal may have an adverse effect 
on the market price of the respective Obligations. See “BOND INSURANCE” herein. 
 
MUNICIPAL ADVISOR 
 
NW Financial Group, LLC, Bloom field, New Jersey (the “Muni cipal Advisor”) served as 
municipal advisor to the Authority with respect to the issuance of the Obligations and assisted in 
matters relating to the planning, structuring and issuance of the Obligations. The Municipal 
Advisor has made no inquiry of any Authority officials or other persons  as to any financial 
information, documents, statements or materials,  and has not independently verified any such 
financial information, documents, st atements or materials that have  been or may be furnished in 
connection with the authorization, issuance or marketing of the Obligations. Accordingly, the  
50 
  
 
 
Municipal Advisor will not express any opinion with resp ect to the accuracy or completeness of 
any such financial information, documents, statements or materials. 
 
The Municipal Advisor is an independent firm and is not engaged in the business of underwriting, 
trading or distributing municipal securities or other public securities. The Municipal Advisor is an 
Independent Registered Municipal Advisor pursuant to the Dodd-Frank Act. 
 
UNDERWRITING 
 
On the date hereof, the Authority entered into an obligation purchase contract (the “Purchase 
Contract”) with Stifel, Nicolaus & Company, In corporated, as underwrite r of the Obligations 
shown on the front cover page hereof (the “U nderwriter”), under which the Underwriter has 
agreed, subject to certain cust omary conditions precedent to clos ing, to purchase the Obligations 
as follows.  
 
The Underwriter will purchase the Tax-Exem pt Water Notes at a purchase price of 
$88,532,021.00, which reflects the principal amount  of $87,370,000.00, plus an original issue 
premium in the amount of $1,260,749.10 less the Underwriter’s discount in the amount of 
$98,728.10.   
 
The Underwriter will purchas e the Taxable Water Notes at a purchase price of $8,914,914.75, 
which reflects the principal amount of $8,925, 000.00, less the Underwriter’s discount in the 
amount of $10,085.25.   
 
The Underwriter will purchase the Sewer Notes at a purchase price of $18,057,006.00, which 
reflects the principal amount of $17,820,000.00, plus an  original issue premium in the amount of 
$257,142.60 less the Underwriter’s discount in the amount of $20,136.60.   
 
The Underwriter will purchase the Water Bonds at a purchase price of $52,784,199.15, which 
reflects the principal amount of $48,065,000.00, plus an  original issue premium in the amount of 
$4,911,459.15, less the Underwriter’s discount in the amount of $192,260.00.   
 
The Underwriter will purchase the Sewer B onds at a purchase price of $86,103,561.90, which 
reflects the principal amount of $78,405,000.00, plus an  original issue premium in the amount of 
$8,012,181.90, less the Underwriter’s discount in the amount of $313,620.00.   
 
The Underwriter will purchase the Taxable Bonds at a purchase price of $9,960,000.00, which 
reflects the principal amount of $10,000,000.00, less the Underwriter’s discount in the amount of 
$40,000.00.   
 
The Underwriter will be obligated to purchase all of the Obligations if any of the Obligations are 
purchased.  The Obligations may be offered and sold to certain dealers (including underwriters 
and other dealers depositing such Obligations into investment trusts) at yields higher/prices lower 
than the public offering yields/prices set forth on the inside front cover page of this Official  
51 
  
 
 
Statement, and such public offering yields/prices  may be changed from time to time by the 
Underwriter. 
 
Stifel and its affiliates comprise a full service financial institution engaged in activities which may 
include sales and trading, commercial and investment banking, advisory, investment management, 
investment research, principal investment, hedging, market making, brokerage and other financial 
and non-financial activities and serv ices.  Stifel and its affiliates may have provided, and may in 
the future provide, a variety of th ese services to the Authority a nd to persons and entities with 
relationships with the Authority, for which they received or will receive customary fees and 
expenses.   
 
In the ordinary course of these business activities , Stifel and its affiliat es may purchase, sell or 
hold a broad array of investment s and actively trade securities , derivatives, loans and other 
financial instruments for their own account and for the accounts of  their customers, and such 
investment and trading activities may involve or re late to assets, securities and/or instruments of 
the Authority (directly, as collat eral securing other obligations or  otherwise) and/or persons and 
entities with relationships with the Authority.   
 
Stifel and its affiliates may also communicate independent invest ment recommendations, market 
color or trading ideas and/or publish or express independent research views in respect of such 
assets, securities or instruments and may at any time hold, or recommend to clients that they should 
acquire such assets, securities and instruments.  Such investment and securities activities may 
involve securities and instruments of the Authority. 
 
LEGALITY FOR INVESTMENT 
 
The Act provides that (a) the St ate and all public officers, m unicipalities, c ounties, political 
subdivisions and public bodies, and agencies thereof; (b) all banks, trust companies, savings banks 
and institutions, building and loan associations, savings a nd loan associations, investment 
companies, and other persons carrying on a banking or investment business, all insurance 
companies, insurance associati ons and other persons carrying on an insurance business, which 
banks, trust companies, and such other instituti ons are organized and existing under the laws of 
the State; and (c) all executors, administrators, guardians, trustees and other fiduciaries acting 
under the laws of the State, may legally invest any sinking funds, moneys or other funds belonging 
to them or within their control in obligations of authorities created pursuant to the Act and such 
obligations will be authorized security for any and all public deposits.

Management is responsible for the accompanying financial statem ents of the Jersey City Municipal Utilities Authority as of 
and for the year ended D ecember 31, 2024, and the related st atements of revenues, expenses and changes in net position and 
cash flows for the year then ended, in accordance with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America.  
We have performed a compilati on engagement in accordance with Statemen ts on Standards for Accounting and Review 
Services promulgated by the Accounting and Review Services Committee of the AICPA.  We did not audit or review the 
financial statements nor were we required to perform any procedures to verify the accuracy or completeness of the information 
provided by management.  We do not express an opinion, a conclusion, nor provide any assurance on these financial statements. 
Management has elected to omit substantially  all the disclosures required by accounting principles generally accepted in the 
United States of America.  If the omitted disclosures were included in the financial statements, they might influence the user’s 
conclusions about the Jersey City Municipal Utilities Authority’s  financial position, results of operations, and cash flows. 
Accordingly, the financial statements are not designed for those who are not informed about such matters. 
Management has omitted management’s discussion and analysis, pension, and other post-employment benefit information that 
accounting principles generally accepted in United States of America requires to be presented to supplement the basic financial 
statements.  Such missing info rmation, although not a required part of the ba sic financial statements, is required by the 
Governmental Accounting Standards Board who considers it to be an essential part of financial reporting for placing the basic 
financials statements in an appropriate operational, economic, or historical context.
